label: Charlie Munger's Enduring Influence
description: The letter emphasizes Charlie Munger's pivotal role as the architect of Berkshire Hathaway's success and Buffett's deep respect for his guidance.
key_quote: In reality, Charlie was the “architect” of the present Berkshire, and I acted as the “general contractor” to carry out the day-by-day construction of his vision.

label: Shareholder Communication Philosophy
description: Berkshire prioritizes direct, honest communication with long-term shareholders, avoiding typical investor relations fluff and focusing on delivering both good and bad news.
key_quote: We cherish their presence and believe they are entitled to hear every year both the good and bad news, delivered directly from their CEO and not from an investor-relations officer or communications consultant forever serving up optimism and syrupy mush.

label: Focus on Operating Earnings
description: The letter stresses the importance of operating earnings as a more accurate reflection of Berkshire's performance than GAAP net earnings, which are distorted by unrealized capital gains/losses.
key_quote: At Berkshire, our view is that “earnings” should be a sensible concept that Bertie will find somewhat useful – but only as a starting point – in evaluating a business.

label: Long-Term Investment Strategy
description: Berkshire's strategy centers on owning businesses with enduring economics and deploying capital at high returns, emphasizing patience and avoiding short-term market fluctuations.
key_quote: Our goal at Berkshire is simple: We want to own either all or a portion of businesses that enjoy good economics that are fundamental and enduring.

label: Challenges of Size and Scale
description: The letter acknowledges that Berkshire's immense size makes it increasingly difficult to find acquisition opportunities that can significantly impact its overall performance.
key_quote: Those days are long behind us; size did us in, though increased competition for purchases was also a factor.

label: Importance of Trustworthy Management
description: The letter highlights the necessity of having able and trustworthy managers running the businesses Berkshire owns, acknowledging past disappointments and the difficulty of judging character.
key_quote: We also hope these favored businesses are run by able and trustworthy managers, though that is a more difficult judgment to make, however, and Berkshire has had its share of disappointments.
